description: Core prescribing cascade knowledge base for clinical decision support — DEV build
disclaimer: For decision support only. Always verify with current clinical guidelines.

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Inhibición de prostaglandinas → retención de sodio/agua → vasoconstricción → elevación de PA
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Prostaglandin inhibition → sodium/water retention + vasoconstriction → BP elevation
cascades.prevalence_es: Muy común; uno de los cascadas más documentadas
cascades.prevalence_en: Very common; one of the most documented cascades
cascades.clinical_note_es: Considerar si los AINEs son realmente necesarios. En PVVIH, preferir paracetamol cuando sea posible.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider if NSAIDs are truly necessary. In PLHIV, prefer paracetamol when possible.
references: Scott IA et al. Med J Aust 2013
Lanas A et al. Aliment Pharmacol Ther 2003

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Inhibición de COX-1 gástrica → reducción de moco protector → irritación/úlcera GI
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Gastric COX-1 inhibition → reduced protective mucus → GI irritation/ulceration
cascades.ade_es: Dispepsia, úlcera péptica, náuseas, dolor abdominal
cascades.ade_en: Dyspepsia, peptic ulcer, nausea, abdominal pain
cascades.prevalence_es: Muy común; los IBP son frecuentemente prescritos innecesariamente
cascades.prevalence_en: Very common; PPIs are frequently prescribed unnecessarily
cascades.clinical_note_es: Si se suspende el AINE, el IBP puede no ser necesario. En PVVIH verificar interacciones de IBP con ARV.
cascades.clinical_note_en: If NSAID is stopped, the PPI may not be needed. In PLHIV verify PPI-ARV interactions.

cascades.name_es: IECA → Tos → Antitusivo / Cambio a ARA-II
cascades.name_en: ACE Inhibitor → Cough → Antitussive / Switch to ARB
cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Acumulación de bradicinina → irritación de vías respiratorias → tos seca
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Bradykinin accumulation → respiratory tract irritation → dry cough
cascades.prevalence_es: 10-15% de pacientes con IECAs desarrollan tos. Más frecuente en mujeres y pacientes asiáticos.
cascades.prevalence_en: 10-15% of patients on ACE inhibitors develop cough. More common in women and Asian patients.
cascades.clinical_note_es: La solución correcta es cambiar el IECA por un ARA-II, no añadir antitusivo.
cascades.clinical_note_en: The correct solution is to switch ACE inhibitor to ARB, not add an antitussive.

cascades.name_es: Antagonista del calcio → Edema maleolar → Diurético
cascades.name_en: Calcium Channel Blocker → Ankle Edema → Diuretic
cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Vasodilatación arteriolar preferente → incremento de presión capilar → edema periférico
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Preferential arteriolar vasodilation → increased capillary pressure → peripheral edema
cascades.prevalence_es: Ocurre en ~10% con amlodipino. El diurético trata el síntoma pero no la causa.
cascades.prevalence_en: Occurs in ~10% with amlodipine. Diuretic treats the symptom but not the cause.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Considerar reducción de dosis del ACa, cambio a dihidropiridina de menor efecto vasodilatador, o adición de IECA/ARA-II.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider reducing CCB dose, switching to less vasodilatory dihydropyridine, or adding ACEi/ARB.

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Resistencia a insulina inducida por glucocorticoides → hiperglucemia
cascades.prevalence_es: Ocurre en ~20-50% de los tratados con corticoides. En PVVIH puede exacerbarse con inhibidores de proteasa.
cascades.prevalence_en: Occurs in ~20-50% treated with corticosteroids. In PLHIV may be exacerbated by protease inhibitors.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Revisar si el corticoide puede reducirse/suspenderse. La insulina suele ser más apropiada que la metformina para la hiperglucemia esteroidea.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Review whether corticosteroid can be reduced/discontinued. Insulin is often more appropriate than metformin for steroid-induced hyperglycemia.
references: Clore JN, Thurby-Hay L. Ann Pharmacother 2009

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Inhibición de osteoblastos + activación de osteoclastos → pérdida ósea
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Osteoblast inhibition + osteoclast activation → bone loss
cascades.prevalence_es: Muy común en uso crónico de corticoides. En PVVIH, la osteoporosis es multifactorial (ARV, corticoides, inflamación crónica).
cascades.prevalence_en: Very common with chronic corticosteroid use. In PLHIV, osteoporosis is multifactorial (ARV, corticosteroids, chronic inflammation).
cascades.clinical_note_es: Puede ser una prescripción apropiada si el corticoide es necesario a largo plazo. Evaluar el contexto.
cascades.clinical_note_en: May be an appropriate prescription if corticosteroid is needed long-term. Evaluate the context.
references: Weinstein RS. Endocrinol Metab Clin North Am 2012

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Reducción de excreción renal de urato → hiperuricemia → ataque de gota
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Reduced renal urate excretion → hyperuricemia → gout attack
cascades.prevalence_es: Las tiazidas aumentan el riesgo de gota en ~50%. Considerar diurético alternativo.
cascades.prevalence_en: Thiazides increase gout risk by ~50%. Consider alternative diuretic.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Si la tiazida puede suspenderse, los niveles de urato podrían normalizarse. Evaluar indicación.
cascades.clinical_note_en: If thiazide can be stopped, urate levels may normalize. Evaluate indication.

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Activación de receptores mu opioides en plexo entérico → reducción del peristaltismo → estreñimiento
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Activation of mu-opioid receptors in enteric plexus → reduced peristalsis → constipation
cascades.prevalence_es: Ocurre en >40% de pacientes con opioides crónicos. El laxante está indicado profilácticamente.
cascades.prevalence_en: Occurs in >40% of patients on chronic opioids. Laxative is indicated prophylactically.
cascades.clinical_note_es: En este caso el laxante puede ser apropiado. Evaluar si el opioide puede reducirse o suspenderse.
cascades.clinical_note_en: In this case the laxative may be appropriate. Evaluate if the opioid can be reduced or stopped.

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Bloqueo dopaminérgico D2 en vía nigroestriada → síntomas extrapiramidales
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: D2 dopaminergic blockade in nigrostriatal pathway → extrapyramidal symptoms
cascades.prevalence_es: Más frecuente con antipsicóticos típicos y altas dosis. Valorar cambio a antipsicótico con menor riesgo EPS.
cascades.prevalence_en: More common with typical antipsychotics and high doses. Consider switching to antipsychotic with lower EPS risk.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Los anticolinérgicos tienen muchos efectos adversos propios (confusión, retención urinaria, glaucoma). Evitar en mayores.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Anticholinergics have many adverse effects themselves (confusion, urinary retention, glaucoma). Avoid in elderly.
references: Kane JM et al. J Clin Psychiatry 2009

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Activación serotoninérgica → estimulación central → insomnio, especialmente al inicio
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Serotonergic activation → central stimulation → insomnia, especially at initiation
cascades.prevalence_es: El insomnio puede ser parte de la depresión subyacente, no solo del ISRS. Contextualizar.
cascades.prevalence_en: Insomnia may be part of the underlying depression, not just the SSRI. Contextualize.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Los hipnóticos tienen riesgo de dependencia. Considerar higiene del sueño, cambio de hora de administración del ISRS o cambio de antidepresivo.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Hypnotics carry dependency risk. Consider sleep hygiene, changing SSRI timing, or switching antidepressant.
references: Fava M et al. J Clin Psychiatry 2006

cascades.name_es: Inhibidor de colinesterasa → Incontinencia urinaria → Anticolinérgico
cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Exceso colinérgico vesical → contracciones involuntarias del detrusor → incontinencia
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: Bladder cholinergic excess → involuntary detrusor contractions → urge incontinence
cascades.ade_es: Incontinencia urinaria de urgencia, urgencia miccional
cascades.prevalence_es: Paradójico: el anticolinérgico antagoniza directamente el efecto del inhibidor de colinesterasa en el SNC.
cascades.prevalence_en: Paradoxical: the anticholinergic directly antagonizes the cholinesterase inhibitor's CNS effect.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Cascada particularmente problemática: el tratamiento de la incontinencia reduce la eficacia del tratamiento del Alzheimer.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Particularly problematic cascade: treating incontinence reduces effectiveness of Alzheimer's treatment.
references: Gill SS et al. Arch Intern Med 2005

cascades.ade_mechanism_es: Alteración de la microbiota intestinal y motilidad GI → diarrea, náuseas, dolor abdominal
cascades.ade_mechanism_en: GI microbiota alteration and motility changes → diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain
cascades.prevalence_es: Los síntomas GI son muy frecuentes con metformina (hasta 30%), especialmente al inicio. Suelen mejorar con el tiempo.
cascades.prevalence_en: GI symptoms are very common with metformin (up to 30%), especially at initiation. Usually improve over time.
cascades.clinical_note_es: Solución preferida: formulación de liberación prolongada o reducción de dosis, no añadir antidiarreico.
cascades.clinical_note_en: Preferred solution: extended-release formulation or dose reduction, not adding an antidiarrheal.

cascades.ade: Depression, low mood, fatigue, emotional blunting
differential_hints: Underlying major depressive disorder independent of beta-blocker
Social isolation or situational psychosocial factors
Chronic heart failure causing fatigue misattributed to depression
Sleep apnea contributing to fatigue and low mood
cascades.clinical_note_en: Evidence for beta-blocker-induced depression is debated; lipophilic agents (propranolol, metoprolol) have higher CNS penetration. Consider switching to a hydrophilic agent (atenolol) before adding an antidepressant.

cascades.ade: Myalgia, muscle weakness, myopathy, rhabdomyolysis (rare)
differential_hints: Hypothyroidism as independent cause (check TSH and CK)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Check CK levels. If statin-induced myalgia confirmed, consider dose reduction, drug holiday, or switch to less myotoxic statin (pravastatin, fluvastatin). In PLHIV, protease inhibitors increase statin exposure—drug interaction is critical.

cascades.ade: Hypokalemia, muscle cramps, weakness, cardiac arrhythmia
differential_hints: Concurrent vomiting or diarrhea as additional potassium loss
cascades.clinical_note_en: Hypomagnesemia must be corrected simultaneously—it prevents effective potassium repletion. Consider addition of a potassium-sparing diuretic instead of oral supplement if ongoing loop diuretic is needed.

cascades.name_en: ACE Inhibitor / ARB → Hyperkalemia → Potassium Binder
cascades.ade: Hyperkalemia, bradycardia, cardiac conduction abnormalities
differential_hints: Concurrent NSAID use reducing renal potassium excretion
Dietary potassium excess or potassium supplement over-supplementation
Trimethoprim-induced hyperkalemia (relevant in PLHIV on TMP-SMX for PCP prophylaxis)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Before adding a potassium binder, reassess ACEi/ARB dose and consider dietary modification. In PLHIV, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (used for PCP prophylaxis) also causes hyperkalemia—additive risk warrants close monitoring.

cascades.ade: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, presyncope, syncope, falls
differential_hints: Volume depletion or dehydration from inadequate fluid intake
Polypharmacy with multiple hypotensive agents (additive effect)
Amyloidosis or other systemic autonomic dysfunction
cascades.clinical_note_en: Dose reduction or medication elimination is preferred over adding vasopressors. Fludrocortisone carries hypokalemia and fluid retention risk; avoid in heart failure. Non-pharmacological measures (head-of-bed elevation, compression stockings, hydration) first.

cascades.ade: Hypothyroidism, fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, bradycardia worsening
differential_hints: Prior thyroid pathology or thyroid surgery
Iodine excess from radiographic contrast agents or diet
Drug-induced thyroiditis from other agents (lithium, interferon)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Amiodarone contains ~37% iodine by weight and has a half-life of 40–55 days; thyroid monitoring is needed for years after discontinuation. Hypothyroidism may be treated while amiodarone continues if arrhythmia control is critical.

cascades.name_en: Benzodiazepine / Z-Drug → Falls / Fractures → Bone Treatment
cascades.ade: Sedation, cognitive impairment, gait instability, falls, hip fractures
differential_hints: Environmental hazards (poor lighting, loose rugs, unsafe bathroom)
Cognitive impairment or dementia as primary fall cause
Vestibular disorder or benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV)
Parkinson's disease or other movement disorder
Muscle weakness from deconditioning, sarcopenia, or vitamin D deficiency
cascades.clinical_note_en: Benzodiazepines and Z-drugs are Beers Criteria drugs in older adults. Gradual tapering and discontinuation is the correct intervention, not treating the downstream fracture risk while perpetuating the cause.

cascades.ade: Urinary retention, overflow incontinence, bladder outlet obstruction
differential_hints: Benign prostatic hyperplasia as independent cause (particularly in older men)
High cumulative anticholinergic burden from multiple medications
Neurogenic bladder from diabetes or spinal cord pathology
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider switching TCA to an SSRI or SNRI with much lower anticholinergic burden. In older men with BPH, TCAs are particularly high-risk. Adding an alpha-blocker perpetuates the anticholinergic cause without addressing it.

cascades.name_en: Gabapentinoid → Dizziness / Falls → Analgesic for Fall Injury
cascades.ade: Dizziness, somnolence, ataxia, peripheral edema, falls
differential_hints: Polypharmacy interactions with other CNS depressants (opioids, benzodiazepines)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Gabapentinoids are increasingly recognized as high-risk in elderly patients, especially at higher doses or with renal impairment. Dose reduction or discontinuation should be considered before treating fall-related injuries with additional analgesics.

cascades.name_en: Atypical Antipsychotic → Metabolic Syndrome / Weight Gain → Metformin
cascades.ade: Weight gain, metabolic syndrome, dyslipidemia, impaired glucose tolerance
differential_hints: Dietary habits and sedentary lifestyle as primary drivers
Pre-existing metabolic syndrome before antipsychotic initiation
Psychiatric illness itself affecting eating regulation
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider switching to a metabolically neutral antipsychotic (aripiprazole, ziprasidone, amisulpride) before adding metabolic agents. In PLHIV, also consider ARV contribution to lipodystrophy and metabolic syndrome.

cascades.name_en: Levodopa / Dopamine Agonist → Orthostatic Hypotension → Vasopressor
cascades.ade: Orthostatic hypotension, syncope, falls, dizziness on standing
differential_hints: Parkinson's disease-related autonomic dysfunction (independent of medications)
Volume depletion or inadequate fluid intake
Dehydration secondary to dysphagia or swallowing difficulties
cascades.clinical_note_en: Parkinson's disease itself causes autonomic dysfunction; disentangling drug effect from disease progression is challenging. Non-pharmacological interventions (head-of-bed elevation, physical countermaneuvers, compression garments) should precede pharmacological treatment.

cascades.name_en: First-Generation Antihistamine → Cognitive Impairment → Cholinesterase Inhibitor
cascades.ade: Anticholinergic-induced cognitive impairment, confusion, delirium, acute memory complaints
differential_hints: Early Alzheimer's disease or other dementia onset
Other anticholinergic medications in polypharmacy (cumulative burden)
Normal cognitive aging misinterpreted as pathological
cascades.clinical_note_en: Particularly harmful cascade: first-generation antihistamines have very high anticholinergic burden and directly antagonize cholinesterase inhibitors. Stopping the antihistamine may fully resolve apparent cognitive decline—a therapeutic trial before prescribing dementia drugs.

cascades.ade: Constipation, bloating, straining, fecal impaction
differential_hints: Dietary fiber deficiency or inadequate fluid intake
Concurrent opioid use as primary or additive cause
Other anticholinergic medications in polypharmacy (cumulative GI effect)
Colorectal pathology (do not miss structural cause)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) as an alternative to anticholinergic urinary agents—similar efficacy with lower constipation and cognitive burden. Cumulative anticholinergic burden across all medications must be assessed in older patients.

cascades.name_en: Alpha-1 Blocker → Orthostatic Hypotension → Vasopressor / Fall Injury Treatment
cascades.ade: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, syncope, falls, fall-related injury
differential_hints: Volume depletion from concurrent diuretic use
Antihypertensive polypharmacy with additive hypotensive effect
BPH as the primary indication driving continued use
Dehydration from warm climate or inadequate intake
cascades.clinical_note_en: Alpha-1 blockers for BPH in older men frequently cause first-dose and positional orthostatic hypotension. Consider 5-alpha reductase inhibitor or beta-3 agonist alternatives. If continued, start at very low dose at bedtime and counsel on rising slowly.

cascades.ade: Delirium, acute confusion, agitation, hallucinations, paranoia
differential_hints: Infection-related delirium (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis)—most common cause in elderly
Underlying dementia with behavioural and psychological symptoms
cascades.clinical_note_en: Identify and remove the offending anticholinergic drug first—this alone may resolve delirium. Antipsychotics for delirium management may be appropriate short-term but have their own anticholinergic burden, potentially worsening the root cause.

cascades.name_en: Loop Diuretic → Urinary Urgency / Nocturia → Anticholinergic
cascades.ade: Urinary urgency, frequency, nocturia (pharmacological diuresis misattributed to overactive bladder)
differential_hints: True overactive bladder disease independent of diuretic
Benign prostatic hyperplasia with urge component
Poorly controlled diabetes mellitus causing osmotic diuresis
cascades.clinical_note_en: Repositioning the time of diuretic administration (e.g., morning dose only, avoiding evening) may resolve nocturia without adding any new medication. Urological evaluation is warranted before starting an anticholinergic agent.

cascades.ade: Hypomagnesemia, muscle cramps, tetany, cardiac arrhythmia, refractory hypokalemia
differential_hints: Loop diuretic co-administration (CORE-038) with additive magnesium wasting
Diabetes mellitus with renal magnesium wasting
cascades.clinical_note_en: FDA issued a safety communication in 2011 about PPI-induced hypomagnesemia. Oral supplementation may be insufficient if PPI is continued; switching from PPI to H2 blocker may be necessary. Co-existing hypokalemia is resistant to correction until magnesium is normalized.

cascades.ade: Vitamin B12 deficiency, macrocytic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive impairment
differential_hints: Pernicious anemia with intrinsic factor deficiency
Crohn's disease or ileal resection impairing absorption
Metformin-induced B12 depletion (additive with PPI, very common in PLHIV)
Atrophic gastritis as primary gastric cause
cascades.clinical_note_en: B12 deficiency from PPIs is more common with long-term use (>2 years) and higher doses. In older adults, neurological manifestations may precede anemia. In PLHIV, B12 deficiency can worsen ARV-related peripheral neuropathy—important cascade to detect.

cascades.ade: Nausea, vomiting, gastroparesis, delayed gastric emptying
differential_hints: Underlying GI pathology (gastric outlet obstruction, gastroparesis)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Opioid-induced nausea typically occurs at initiation and often resolves within days—watchful waiting is appropriate. Metoclopramide may worsen EPS risk in patients already on antipsychotics. In PLHIV, antiretroviral-related nausea may compound opioid-induced nausea.

cascades.name_en: Oral Iron Supplement → Constipation / GI Upset → Laxative or Antacid
cascades.ade: Constipation, nausea, abdominal cramps, dark stools, GI upset
differential_hints: Underlying constipation from other causes (opioids, anticholinergics)
Celiac disease or GI malabsorption causing GI symptoms
Colorectal cancer (important not to miss in new GI symptoms)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider alternative iron formulations (ferric carboxymaltose IV, alternate-day oral dosing, lower-dose formulations) to reduce GI side effects. Adding antacids or PPIs paradoxically reduces iron absorption, defeating the purpose of supplementation.

cascades.name_en: Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic → C. difficile Diarrhea → Metronidazole / Vancomycin
cascades.ade: Clostridioides difficile infection, pseudomembranous colitis, watery diarrhea, colonic inflammation
differential_hints: IBD exacerbation (Crohn's colitis or UC flare)
Ischemic colitis (consider in patients with vascular risk factors)
Enteric infection from other pathogen (Salmonella, Campylobacter)
cascades.clinical_note_en: C. difficile must be confirmed by stool toxin or PCR before treating. In PLHIV, C. difficile is more common and may be more severe. Oral vancomycin is now first-line over metronidazole; fidaxomicin preferred for recurrent cases. Stop the precipitating antibiotic if clinically feasible.

cascades.ade: Hypertension, sodium and water retention, elevated cardiovascular risk
differential_hints: Essential hypertension that was poorly controlled before corticosteroid
Pain-related or anxiety-related blood pressure elevation
Obstructive sleep apnea as an independent cause
NSAID co-administration further worsening blood pressure
cascades.clinical_note_en: Distinct from CC005 (hyperglycemia cascade). Corticosteroid-induced hypertension is dose-dependent; fludrocortisone has the greatest mineralocorticoid effect. Corticosteroid dose minimization is preferred before adding antihypertensive. ACEi/ARB preferred if microalbuminuria is present.

cascades.name_en: Corticosteroid → Peripheral Edema / Fluid Retention → Diuretic
cascades.ade: Peripheral edema, fluid retention, weight gain
cascades.clinical_note_en: Edema from corticosteroids is often dose-dependent. Sodium restriction and corticosteroid dose minimization are preferred over adding diuretics. Adding a diuretic increases hypokalemia risk, especially if patient is also on a loop diuretic.

cascades.ade: Hyperlipidemia, hypertriglyceridemia, increased cardiovascular risk
differential_hints: Dietary habits and sedentary lifestyle as dominant factors
cascades.clinical_note_en: Consider switching to a metabolically neutral antipsychotic (aripiprazole, amisulpride) before adding a statin. In PLHIV, statin-ARV interactions are critical: simvastatin is contraindicated with PIs; pravastatin or rosuvastatin are preferred.

cascades.name_en: Thiazide → Hyponatremia → Sodium Supplement / Fluid Management
cascades.ade: Hyponatremia, lethargy, cognitive impairment, falls, seizures (severe cases)
differential_hints: SIADH from other medications or CNS/pulmonary disease
SSRI/SNRI co-administration (major additive SIADH risk, see CORE-039)
cascades.clinical_note_en: Thiazide-induced hyponatremia is most common in elderly women and often occurs within first weeks of starting or dose increase. Discontinuing the thiazide is the primary intervention. Avoid rapid correction (>8 mEq/L per 24h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.

cascades.ade: Hypomagnesemia, muscle cramps, refractory hypokalemia, cardiac arrhythmia
differential_hints: Alcoholism or chronic alcohol use disorder
PPI co-administration (CORE-029) with additive magnesium wasting effect
Diabetes mellitus with renal tubular magnesium wasting
Chronic diarrhea or short bowel syndrome
cascades.clinical_note_en: Hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia—both electrolytes must be corrected together. Loop diuretics are the most common drug cause of hypomagnesemia. Potassium-sparing diuretics (amiloride) also spare magnesium and may be preferred where clinically appropriate.

cascades.ade: SIADH-mediated hyponatremia, confusion, cognitive impairment, falls, seizures
differential_hints: Thiazide diuretic co-administration (major additive risk, CORE-037)
CNS disease causing SIADH (stroke, tumor, meningitis)
Pulmonary disease causing SIADH (pneumonia, TB, SCLC)
cascades.clinical_note_en: SSRI-induced hyponatremia is most common in elderly patients, especially within the first weeks of starting or dose increase. Paroxetine carries additional anticholinergic risk. Concurrent thiazide greatly amplifies hyponatremia risk. Antidepressant dose reduction or switch is the primary intervention.

cascades.name_en: Enzyme-Inducing Antiepileptic → Vitamin D Deficiency / Osteomalacia → Vitamin D Supplement
cascades.ade: Vitamin D deficiency, osteomalacia, osteoporosis, increased fracture risk
differential_hints: Dietary vitamin D deficiency from inadequate intake or sun exposure
Malabsorption syndrome (celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease)
Age-related decline in cutaneous vitamin D synthesis
cascades.clinical_note_en: Enzyme-inducing antiepileptics accelerate vitamin D catabolism via CYP450 induction. Bone mineral density monitoring is recommended in long-term users. In PLHIV, these antiepileptics may also accelerate ARV metabolism (especially NNRTIs, PIs), creating complex pharmacokinetic drug interactions.
